item.title: Are some of them immortal?
item.content:encoded:
So, they somehow remember their pasts of 10000 years past, does the memory of their extinction became somehow an instict of theirs? Or they were telling their children about the Day of the Flowers?
Did another organization like Chaos Insurgency or Serpent's Hand remind them? Or maybe some elite of them had access to something like Scp-006, they hide and now those elites get to lead their people again?

item.title: Brilliant!
item.content:encoded: I'm taking the enterprise of reading every SCP from 001 on, so I'm just at (less than) 20%, but till now this is one of the most amazing examples of world building here!

item.title: Re: The Power of the SCP
item.content:encoded:
That's an odd way to judge SCPs. This isn't a video game where balance and tiers matter; in most other fiction the only thing that matters in terms of "power level" is the potential for an overpowered character to take all the tension out by making the outcome of every conflict obvious from the start. That isn't the case here.

The bit you're questioning isn't even true.

There is no such thing as the "anomalous pseudo-disease" referred to as SCP-1000-f1. SCP-1000 does not possess a magical death aura. In fact, SCP-1000 does not directly exhibit any anomalous effect whatsoever.

item.title: The Power of the SCP
item.content:encoded: [Deleted]

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: bigfoot_patterson01-new.png
Author(s): Waldo93, Tim Evanson, Shaggydredlocks
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Shaggydredlocks utilizing the images below.

Source: Pixabay
License: CC0
Title: N/A
Author: Waldo93
Note: Pixabay moved to its own Pixabay License in January 2019, but CC0 is still applied to images uploaded before January 2019. See here for details.

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Title: Lucy model - australopithecus afarensis- Smithsonian Museum of Natural History - 2012-05-17
Author: Tim Evanson

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't realize I had already commented in the discussions about this article. I did a recent reread and I just wanted to make sure my appreciation was known.

item.title: Neat
item.content:encoded:
Not much to say but I like this. There may be similarities with the Planet of the Apes but I never watched it so I can't say.

I do wonder why genocide is unfeasible (though unseemly, it seems like something the Foundation would consider), especially since SCP-1000 apparently has no anomalous characteristics other than human-level intelligence. And I wonder how the Foundation responded to their attempt at communication.

Also, I think there's a typo here:

Log 1000-ad065-x1: The following is a rough translation of recent SCP-1000 attempt at communication

Should be "a rough translation of a recent SCP-1000 attempt at communication".

I just noticed that I never +1'ed this one.
Heck, and it's for sure in my top 5 articles on all the wiki, including tales. I did it now.

Re-reading it now tho a question came to my mind: I didn't exactly get what the reference to 2000 is about. Or better, I am not sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is amazing in how it includes a more intricate meaning towards the topic along with providing background information on the GOIs intertwined with SCP-1000.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, is this the ultimate origin of the Birdphone?
MMmm …This scp is so good mmmmmmmmmmmmmmmMMMMMMmmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just means, statistically, that if a human views SCP-1000 for 20 minutes, there's a 3% chance of them dying at any point within those 20 minutes. The chance slowly increases. There's a 4% chance that they will die at any point during the first 40 minutes, then a 5% chance that they will die at any point during the first 60 minutes, and so on.

You're entirely right that actually determining that pattern would require killing quite a lot of people.

But, yes, it's also a completely made-up effect in-universe.

EDIT: Math error.
Big foot got big votes. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like Skips based on mythology/cryptozoology. This is written really well. I love it. Definetely deserved to win the 1000 contest. Nice job.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You do know that part is just a bunch of bullshit, right? If you read the Level 3 documents you'll see that was just a distraction. There is no magical death aura, there were no tests to learn more about SCP-1000-f1 because it does not exist. It's just a cover story.

I know I'm answering to an old comment, but I believe it is meaningful information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little confused by this:

"… has a minimum 2% chance of being instantly killed through anomalous means…. the longer a human views SCP-1000, the higher the chance of instantaneous death increases, at a rate of +1% chance per 20 minutes of viewing…"

How frequently is this chance applied? How often are the dice rolled, so to speak? Do you have a 2%+ chance to instantly die upon first sight, and then if you don't you're fine until 20 minutes pass, at which point you may or may not die again, etc.? Or is it a 2%+ chance every second/minute/?? you're looking at one of these things? Does the chance stick around, or do you top being in danger once you look away?

Side note: I can't even imagine the amount of testing and data that would have been required to discover this pattern!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For two full years I didn't even know that this skip was something other than a bigfoot that kills you if you look at it.

holy shit that's amazing

item.title: Re: Would Humanity be able to defend itself?
item.content:encoded:
we have weaponry that can successfully wipe out 100% of all life on Earth many times over.

So did they. It's just not what happened.

Us getting ahold of their technology obviously wasn't that much of a problem

It was. That's why we needed an intermediary (or, so the story goes).

Rest of long post collapsed
– hide block

Even if the Sasquatch managed to take control over Americas nukes

They wouldn't be able to, and if they reached the point where they could, they wouldn't need to. That's not really what the Foundation is concerned about.

Growing planes out of bushes is great and all, but how would they even attack us with technology like that? OH NO, he's shooting berries at us, RUUUUUUUUN! A fighter plane would take care of their planes in seconds because of it's weak structural integrity.

While this is super funny, it's not quite right. They weren't made out of the bushes or berries that you have in mind. Not any more than our fighter jets are made out of pebbles that you can just kick over, or metal spoons that you can just bend. Rocks and metal are being used in very different ways.

The Foundation alone could take care of them with all the anomalies at their disposal.

SCP-1000 essentially lived with anomalies as an entire species. They didn't hide them away and allow only a few people to deal with them, the way humans do. There's a total perspective shift missing here.

Heck, all they have to do is make a portable Scranton Reality Anchor and they can't touch you!

Not quite what reality anchors do (and assumes a lot re: what non-humans can theoretically do to get around reality anchors).

Humanities arsenal of anomalous weaponry would completely OBLITERATE the opposition in a matter of seconds if we wished it so.

Not 'if we wished it so' — rather, 'if we could control it well enough'. Which we can't. Also, 1000 was far better at controlling anomalies than we are, and look where it got them. This would be fun to read about — I'd love to see those tales — but not likely to turn out well for either side. This is part of what the Foundation is afraid of. Also, keep in mind some of our anomalies are inherited in some part from 1000.

(of course, 1000 would dispute the entire 'anomaly vs normalcy' paradigm, but that's an aside)

there's NO WAY they could kill all of us before people were […] somehow kept safe through various anomalous means.

True. But this also applied/applies to 1000.

Overall, 1000's tech would spank our modern tech, and no one (including the Foundation) knows how using anomalies would work out. But 1000 doesn't have an active civilization to use their tech or even make it, if applicable (the Foundation is unclear what kind of access they have, and what memories or knowledge they even have).

Like, you may know how to make an AK-47, but that doesn't mean you have access to gunpowder, or a factory to make the parts. Worse, maybe you just know how to use an AK-47, but don't know how to make one! Heck, I live in a society where AK-47s are common, and I'm not 100% sure I could use one correctly.

Importantly, though, any war between 1000 and the Foundation would quickly get way more complicated once the rest of the anomalous world joined in, which would probably be immediately. (A war between 1000 and humanity would pretty much not happen, not in this universe anyway.)

This was a fun comment to reply to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, SCP-1000 kind of reminds me of the morlocks (The Time Machine, H. G. Wells). This is really cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a person who loves myths and I have seen dozens of Bigfoot photos and after reading this I am never going to see another Bigfoot pic again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats on the Volgun Reading

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now we need to catch yeti and finally, we will try to catch harambe reincarnation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now we need to catch yeti and finally, we will try to catch harambe reincarnation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice work, moose. This definitely deserved the slot, and it's just a great skip in general.

I remember reading this when I was 13 and was addicted to Series I skips. I saw this skip, read the Description, thought "huh, neat" and skipped the level three letter. For two full years I didn't even know that this skip was something other than a bigfoot that kills you if you look at it.

Seriously, though, +1.

item.title: Would Humanity be able to defend itself?
item.content:encoded: The apes may still have access to their seemingly unstoppable weaponry, but when put into comparison to the weaponry humans possess outside of the foundation lore, we would definitely stand a chance I think. They had weaponry that could successfully wipe out 70% of their population, but we have weaponry that can successfully wipe out 100% of all life on Earth many times over. Us getting ahold of their technology obviously wasn't that much of a problem, but those apes getting their hands on our Nukes is a different story. Even if the Sasquatch managed to take control over Americas nukes, it would instantly turn into a M.A.D. situation and one side would have to back down or everyone dies. Growing planes out of bushes is great and all, but how would they even attack us with technology like that? OH NO, he's shooting berries at us, RUUUUUUUUN! A fighter plane would take care of their planes in seconds because of it's weak structural integrity. Or you can just stick your cigarette butt on it, that would just catch it on fire and it would be down for the count. Now putting it into the perspective of the SCP universe, the apes wouldn't stand a chance! The Foundation alone could take care of them with all the anomalies at their disposal. Heck, all they have to do is make a portable Scranton Reality Anchor and they can't touch you! Humanities arsenal of anomalous weaponry would completely OBLITERATE the opposition in a matter of seconds if we wished it so. Even if they beat us in the war (which would never happen), there's NO WAY they could kill all of us before people were quarantined in male and female pairs into different SCPs or somehow kept safe through various anomalous means. They would literally have to search through every dimension in a multiverse of infinite dimensions just to get the last of us, but in reality they kinda can't, mainly because we'll just take the theoretical key with us to the other side. I don't think the apes would stand a chance whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Truly, The most terrifying SCP on the site.

item.title: Cross Linking
item.content:encoded: If cross-linking is still going on, wouldn't SCP-2636 fit into the cyclical theme?

Woot! Congratulations, thedeadlymoose, Bigfoot reached +700.

/confetti

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
– hide block

Maybe this whole Bloom thing happened a bunch of times, who knows?

Said by someone else about something totally different:

Why did we have to build this thing?
When did we do it?
How long have we been doing it?
Do we even know?!

One of the reasons I love SCP-2000 is that it — totally on accident — keyed in really hard to some of the themes I intended in this SCP (but typically, only barely implied in the text, at least until the recent crosslink additions).

What does it mean to have everything you've ever known erased as if it never were? What would it mean if it could happen again? If it's a good thing — a good thing for whom? If you think it's you, are you sure? Why?

I also love Ophite's work (Buried Giant may be the most perfect SCP on the site), and I will think about crosslinking these. Will need to re-read.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Hey what about the Descensus civilization (SCP-1348, SCP-1427)? They are immensely similar in concept to SCP-1000, being a humanlike pre-human civilization who were destroyed by humanity with the help of a rogue god (in the case of the Descensi, it was the Elohim god Yehom).

I asked author Ophite about the connection. He said this:
"My weakly-thought-out headcanon is that the war between Bigfoot and Decensus happened relatively early on in Decensus history. Before that, there wasn't much contact between the two species: Bigfoot mostly kept to boreal forests in North America, Europe, and Asia, whereas human and near-human populations were largely in North and East Africa."

Idk how much the timelines line up since the Day of Flowers happened 10-15000 years ago while the Descensus civilization was mostly wiped out some 70-77000 years ago. Except the Bloom seems to rewrite time and everything. I don't even know. The Descensus were created some 2 million years ago yet had some technology from 12 million years ago. Maybe this whole Bloom thing happened a bunch of times, who knows?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Added link to 2511 as well. Also made a bunch more tweaks.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Links! LINKS!! AND MORE LINKS!!! More links than Hyrule has room for!

Huaaauugghhhh.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2232 doesn't appear to be Bigfoot tech, although it's great in the in-joke sense. It's just a literal Android phone that is a bird. (Also adorable)

I can immediately think of a way of working it in, but there isn't already an existing connection, so it's lower priority. I'll likely only do it if the author (minmin) thinks that making a direct connection semi-canon (1000 is a juggernaut and people might tend to assume its content is automatically True) to that SCP would be cool to do.

(or if I suddenly change my mind and just do it)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
2735 are remnants of a pre-human civilization, which is thematically similar to 1000 and appears to be aligned with the cyclical history thing you're hinting at here.

Ah, I see! I'm actually expunging something different in there. 2735 isn't representative of the, er, type of cycles I'm referring to there — it's just a civilization that predates 1000. That's the same thematic cycle, but that sentence means something else…

(There's more stuff I have to write about 1000 eventually, which will eventually be linked there and might clear up more, or outright tell the reader.)

EDIT: Made some tweaks that might clarify.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2232 is Bigfoot tech, yea? You've got the perfect spot for it as an in-line link.

they made things equivalent to cell phones

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
2735 are remnants of a pre-human civilization, which is thematically similar to 1000 and appears to be aligned with the cyclical history thing you're hinting at here. But 2735 is weird biology while 1000 is more classic cryptozoology, so it's your call whether 2735 ought to be linked to this narrative.

I don't have any more suggestions and the new links add a lot to the narrative, so I'll leave y'all to it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Links! LINKS!! AND MORE LINKS!!! More links than Hyrule has room for!

In all seriousness, these are all pretty awesome moose. Since you've already added the links I'm going to go ahead and pull the timer.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Edit: not a big fan of the expungement honestly. But I never am, so meh.

Expungement is core to this SCP's concept, but I don't think my first drafts of expungement are ever good (I've already tweaked it), so suggestions are welcome.

I don't see the connection between SCP-2735 and SCP-1000, but if it's clarified to me, I will certainly consider it. It could be worked in, certainly, but it might feel tacked on given that the other connections I added are pretty core to the SCP's narrative; 2735 shares its themes and is definitely cross-compatible (I even know what I'd write for it, aka, "1000 made use of some tech associated with 2735") but may be incidental to its narrative.

I will work up a link to 2511 because it is very much compatible with 1000's narrative and core themes.

I'll take a look at these others, but since I haven't read them previously, it may take longer.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I did not remotely mean to edit your post by way of replying to it, I was just already clicking the edit button like mad. My apologies.

I am taking the liberty of putting your original comment under a collapsible to avoid spoilers.

Thank you for pointing this out!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
What's that one, it was inspired by a throwaway line from Harry Potter? That would actually be a really good crosslink.

That's SCP-2511.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
What's that one, it was inspired by a throwaway line from Harry Potter? That would actually be a really good crosslink.

And moose, if you add that addendum, SCP-2735 could also be worked in there.

Edit: not a big fan of the expungement honestly. But I never am, so meh.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler
– hide block

underneath the [[SCP-2000|structure]]]

Erm. I think you borked it, moose.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I wish to make an addendum where crosslinks can go for this article. I would like to link as many related articles as possible, however.

I've been holding off because I was gonna try to think of something super creative, but that will have to wait. I'm gonna go ahead and edit in a "good enough" section now.

EDIT: [Removed duplicate text of additions because I just keep tweaking them for more clarity/better writing/etc.]

Open to critique on these additions, regardless of whether they affect the rating. I am adding crosslinks here, but I can edit the style/type/etc. Don't want to bloat the piece.

I'm willing to add a link to any sufficiently well-rated piece on SCP-1000 in here, whether it's a tale or SCP or whatever. Just tell me about it in this thread.

Also, I added that in-line link to the Hand hub.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Therefore, may I suggest:

Organic technology.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: SCP-2273 is Bigfoot!tech.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Edits made by article author. See below for details. Timer removed early.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Yes. Not even SCP-1000 is safe from the Grand Crosslinking (mainly because moose volunteered it as such, but I digress). In addition to an inline link to the Serpent's Hand Hub, there is one other inline link I think would really work well in this article, and that is the line in the Document Alpha-1596-1000 in which they discuss Bigfoot's organic tech. Turns out someone wrote an article about this organic tech in SCP-2932. As such, I am proposing the following edits:

Changing this:

They made things that we still can't comprehend, even though we've thoroughly studied the surviving pieces. Organic technology. They made trees and birds of prey grow into fast-moving ships, herds of animals that became trains, bushes that became flying vehicles. From insects and pigeons they made things equivalent to cell phones, televisions, computers. Atomic bombs. The Children describe vast shining cities, stretching across glaciers and penetrating the deepest caverns, grown skyships of ivory and spider-silk, creatures tending them with hundreds of blinking eyes.

To this:

They made things that we still can't comprehend, even though we've thoroughly studied the surviving pieces. Organic technology. They made trees and birds of prey grow into fast-moving ships, herds of animals that became trains, bushes that became flying vehicles. From insects and pigeons they made things equivalent to cell phones, televisions, computers. Atomic bombs. The Children describe vast shining cities, stretching across glaciers and penetrating the deepest caverns, grown skyships of ivory and spider-silk, creatures tending them with hundreds of blinking eyes.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The worst thing is that given the Foundation's nature, they'll probably want to keep SCP-1000 hidden.
Scratch that, the worst thing is that the trickster forest god that gave humanity the edge might do the same for gorillas or dolphins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the implication that the bigfeet are not necessarily vengeful, in the same way that humanity have splintered over the right response to SCPs. I think if this article had ended before that last note, it would be the weaker for it.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Note (Open)
item.content:encoded:
Noting that I have reverted a recent edit to this article by user Scpcreator1224. The nature of said edit is the following words at the end of the article.

>End transmition

Please note that this constitutes a content edit. Non-authors for non-collaboration articles are only allowed to make edits in terms of grammar, punctuation and spelling. Adding or removing content requires permission from the author. If you feel a certain content edit is necessary, bring it up to the author. But note that the author has final say in the content of their works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my headcanon for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given all the guff about antimemetics, perhaps the thing we turned on ourselves was an antimemetic bomb…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason in this case is that the "cover story" is intentionally bad (in- and out-of-universe) in a way that makes the SCP not work as a story (out of universe) without the letter.

Also because it's not actually the final word on the SCP. The final words on the SCP are hinted by the expunged addendum and is meant to be developed more either in the reader's head or in follow-up work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oddly Heartwarming

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we forgive you;
given choice for now, not forever;
let us back in

MY HEART :*(

I really want to write a tale about a future where they live together with humans again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These things (i.e. stylistics) are up to respective authors to decide. If they feel a collapsible will do, collapsible it is. If not, no collapsible will suffice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a question (IDK if it has been asked before): why isn't the final "letter" hidden in a collapsible? Usually "plot twists" are hidden to be spoilerproof (see SCP-1425, SCP-1055).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I regret ever putting these comments here. I'm sorry to all the people I've wronged. Good bye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yoooo, Bigfoot? Really? Oh yes, this was good despite my initial laughter. Very nice and thorough with a lot of thought put in. Mwah mwah, good work, bud.
